title: A Family Discussion About Moving Abroad
summary: A family — Mum, Dad, and their teenage daughter Zoe — discuss a possible move from the United States to Portugal, weighing the financial benefits, quality of life, and the emotional costs of uprooting their lives.

body.speaker: Mum
body.text: I want us to talk properly about the Portugal idea — not just in passing, but actually sit down and think through what it would mean for all three of us.

body.speaker: Dad
body.text: I think it's worth a serious conversation. The cost of living there is genuinely significantly lower, and with both of us working remotely now, we're not tied to a location the way we were.

body.speaker: Daughter
body.text: Can I just say upfront that I'm not exactly thrilled? I know you've both been excited about this for months, but I feel like my perspective kind of got lost somewhere.

body.speaker: Mum
body.text: That's exactly why we're having this conversation, Zoe. Your perspective matters a great deal. What's your biggest concern?

body.speaker: Daughter
body.text: Honestly? Leaving my friends. I know that sounds small compared to all the things you're talking about, but those friendships took years to build, and I'm supposed to start junior year in September.

body.speaker: Dad
body.text: I hear you, and I don't want to minimise that. But you've also said yourself that you'd like to study in Europe eventually. This could be an extraordinary foundation for that — living somewhere new, learning a language, building a different kind of worldview.

body.speaker: Daughter
body.text: Learning a language and uprooting my entire social life are two very different things. You can learn a language without moving countries.

body.speaker: Mum
body.text: That's true, and I don't think the language point is the strongest argument. But the quality of life question is real. The stress levels we've both been carrying here — the housing costs, the healthcare — they've been affecting all of us, including you.

body.speaker: Daughter
body.text: I know you're stressed. I see it. But is moving to another continent really the answer, or is it just running away from problems that might follow you anyway?

body.speaker: Dad
body.text: That's a genuinely perceptive question, and I've asked myself the same thing. I think the honest answer is: some problems would follow us and some wouldn't. The financial pressure is structural — it's tied to this specific housing market. That part would actually change.

body.speaker: Mum
body.text: We'd be able to save properly for the first time in years. Not just scrape by, but actually build something. That matters for all of us long-term — including your university options, Zoe.

body.speaker: Daughter
body.text: Okay, so let's say I entertain the idea. What would it actually look like? Would I go to a local school or an international school? Would I be doing my final years in a completely foreign education system?

body.speaker: Dad
body.text: That's something we've been researching. There's a well-regarded international school about twenty minutes from the area we're looking at. It uses an American curriculum, so your credits would transfer and your college applications wouldn't be disrupted.

body.speaker: Daughter
body.text: That's actually better than I expected. I assumed I'd have to do the Portuguese system from scratch.

body.speaker: Mum
body.text: We wouldn't do something that set you back academically. That would defeat the purpose. The international school community also tends to be quite close-knit — lots of kids who've moved around and understand what that's like.

body.speaker: Daughter
body.text: So they'd all be weirdly well-travelled and slightly displaced like me. That's either going to be a perfect social fit or completely overwhelming.

body.speaker: Dad
body.text: Probably a bit of both, honestly. I'm not going to pretend it'll be easy. But the things that are hard initially often become the things you look back on as having defined you.

body.speaker: Daughter
body.text: I want to actually visit before any decision is made. I'm not saying yes or no yet, but I need to see the place, see the school, and feel whether it's somewhere I could actually imagine living. Can we do that?

body.speaker: Mum
body.text: Absolutely. That was always part of the plan. We were thinking of going over the school holidays in October — that gives us enough time to visit the school, meet some families, and get a real sense of the place rather than just looking at it online.

body.speaker: Dad
body.text: And Zoe — whatever we decide, we decide together. This isn't something we'd do without you genuinely being on board. That's a promise.

annotations.meaningVi: tình cờ, qua loa, không nghiêm túc
annotations.exampleEn: She mentioned the idea in passing but didn't elaborate.

annotations.meaningVi: không thực sự hào hứng, không mấy vui mừng
annotations.exampleEn: He was not exactly thrilled about the last-minute change of plans.

annotations.meaningVi: bị bỏ qua, không được chú ý đến
annotations.exampleEn: Her concerns kind of got lost in all the excitement about the new project.

annotations.meaningVi: nhổ bật toàn bộ đời sống xã hội, phá vỡ tất cả các mối quan hệ
annotations.exampleEn: Moving at sixteen means uprooting your entire social life at the worst possible time.

annotations.meaningVi: nền tảng phi thường, cơ sở xuất sắc cho sự phát triển
annotations.exampleEn: Living abroad as a teenager gave him an extraordinary foundation for understanding different cultures.

annotations.meaningVi: thế giới quan, cách nhìn về thế giới
annotations.exampleEn: Studying philosophy broadened her worldview considerably.

annotations.meaningVi: chạy trốn khỏi vấn đề, không đối mặt với vấn đề
annotations.exampleEn: He accused them of running away from problems that needed to be solved, not avoided.

annotations.meaningVi: câu hỏi sâu sắc, nhận thức rõ ràng
annotations.exampleEn: The interviewer asked a perceptive question that the candidate hadn't prepared for.

annotations.meaningVi: sống tằn tiện, vừa đủ sống
annotations.exampleEn: On her salary, she could only just scrape by in the city.

annotations.meaningVi: xem xét khả năng, suy nghĩ đến khả năng một điều gì đó
annotations.exampleEn: He wasn't ready to entertain the idea of leaving his hometown.

annotations.meaningVi: được đánh giá cao, có tiếng tốt
annotations.exampleEn: She was accepted into a well-regarded university on the East Coast.

annotations.meaningVi: các tín chỉ học tập được công nhận và chuyển sang hệ thống khác
annotations.exampleEn: She confirmed that her credits would transfer to the new school without any loss.

annotations.meaningVi: phá vỡ mục đích ban đầu, làm ngược lại điều muốn đạt được
annotations.exampleEn: Taking out a loan to pay off a smaller debt would defeat the purpose.

annotations.meaningVi: bị dịch chuyển, không có chỗ đứng ổn định
annotations.exampleEn: Children who have been displaced by family moves often bond quickly with others in the same situation.

annotations.meaningVi: định hình bạn, tạo ra con người bạn
annotations.exampleEn: The challenges she overcame in her twenties ultimately defined her as a person.

annotations.meaningVi: đồng ý và tham gia, ủng hộ kế hoạch
annotations.exampleEn: The whole team needs to be on board before we commit to this direction.
Rising housing costs are forcing many families out of major cities.

annotations.meaningVi: hồ sơ xin vào đại học
annotations.exampleEn: She started working on her college applications a year before the deadline.

annotations.phrase: get a real sense of the place
annotations.meaningVi: cảm nhận thực sự về một nơi, không chỉ qua thông tin
annotations.exampleEn: You need to visit to get a real sense of the place — photographs don't capture everything.

grammarNotes.title: Hedged conditionals in family negotiation: 'let's say I entertain the idea'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Khi một người không sẵn sàng cam kết hoàn toàn với một ý tưởng, họ thường dùng các điều kiện giả định nhẹ nhàng như 'Let's say I entertain the idea' (Giả sử tôi cân nhắc ý tưởng này) hoặc 'If we were to do this' (Nếu chúng ta làm điều này). Cấu trúc này cho phép khám phá khả năng mà không cần xác nhận. Đây là đặc điểm quan trọng của ngôn ngữ đàm phán trong gia đình và cuộc sống hàng ngày ở B2+.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: When a speaker is not ready to commit but is willing to explore, they use tentative conditionals: 'Let's say I entertain the idea' (hypothetical exploration without commitment); 'I'm not saying yes or no yet' (suspension of judgment). This language is characteristic of family negotiation, where emotional stakes are high and positions are fluid.

grammarNotes.title: Reported and indirect speech in family dialogue
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong hội thoại gia đình, người nói thường dùng báo cáo lời nói gián tiếp để tham chiếu đến những gì đã được nói trước đây: 'You've also said yourself that you'd like to study in Europe eventually.' Cấu trúc này vừa nhắc lại thông tin, vừa tạo cảm giác người nghe đã đưa ra tiền đề này trước, làm cho lập luận có tính ràng buộc hơn về mặt tâm lý.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Family dialogue frequently uses reported speech to reference earlier conversations and build arguments: 'You've also said yourself that you'd like to study in Europe eventually.' This structure reminds the listener of their own earlier statement, making it harder to dismiss. Recognising indirect speech and how it is used rhetorically is a key B2+ skill.

translationVi:
Mum: Tôi muốn chúng ta nói chuyện đúng nghĩa về ý tưởng Bồ Đào Nha — không chỉ qua loa, mà thực sự ngồi xuống và suy nghĩ về ý nghĩa của nó đối với cả ba người chúng ta.

Dad: Tôi nghĩ đây là cuộc trò chuyện đáng nghiêm túc. Chi phí sinh hoạt ở đó thực sự thấp hơn đáng kể, và với cả hai chúng ta làm việc từ xa bây giờ, chúng ta không bị ràng buộc với một địa điểm như trước kia.

Daughter: Tôi chỉ muốn nói thẳng là tôi không thực sự hào hứng. Tôi biết cả hai đã hứng khởi với điều này mấy tháng nay, nhưng tôi cảm thấy quan điểm của mình bị bỏ qua đâu đó.

Mum: Đó chính xác là lý do chúng ta có cuộc trò chuyện này, Zoe. Quan điểm của con rất quan trọng. Mối lo ngại lớn nhất của con là gì?

Daughter: Thành thật mà nói? Phải rời bỏ bạn bè. Con biết điều đó nghe có vẻ nhỏ nhặt so với tất cả những thứ bố mẹ đang nói, nhưng những tình bạn đó mất nhiều năm mới gây dựng được, và con sẽ bắt đầu năm học junior vào tháng Chín.

Dad: Ba hiểu, và ba không muốn giảm nhẹ điều đó. Nhưng con cũng đã nói rằng con muốn học ở châu Âu. Đây có thể là nền tảng phi thường cho điều đó — sống ở nơi mới, học một ngôn ngữ, xây dựng một thế giới quan khác.

Daughter: Học một ngôn ngữ và nhổ bật toàn bộ đời sống xã hội của con là hai điều rất khác nhau. Con có thể học ngôn ngữ mà không cần chuyển sang nước khác.

Mum: Điều đó đúng, và mẹ không nghĩ lập luận về ngôn ngữ là thuyết phục nhất. Nhưng câu hỏi về chất lượng cuộc sống là thực tế. Mức độ căng thẳng mà cả hai chúng ta đang gánh chịu ở đây — chi phí nhà ở, chăm sóc sức khỏe — đã ảnh hưởng đến tất cả chúng ta, kể cả con.

Daughter: Con biết bố mẹ căng thẳng. Con thấy điều đó. Nhưng liệu chuyển sang một lục địa khác có thực sự là câu trả lời không, hay chỉ là chạy trốn khỏi những vấn đề có thể theo bố mẹ dù sao đi nữa?

Dad: Đó là câu hỏi thực sự sâu sắc, và ba đã tự hỏi mình điều tương tự. Ba nghĩ câu trả lời trung thực là: một số vấn đề sẽ theo chúng ta và một số sẽ không. Áp lực tài chính mang tính cấu trúc — nó gắn với thị trường nhà ở cụ thể này. Phần đó thực sự sẽ thay đổi.

Mum: Chúng ta có thể tiết kiệm đúng nghĩa lần đầu tiên trong nhiều năm. Không chỉ tằn tiện qua ngày, mà thực sự xây dựng điều gì đó. Điều đó quan trọng với tất cả chúng ta trong dài hạn — kể cả các lựa chọn đại học của con, Zoe.

Daughter: Được rồi, giả sử con cân nhắc ý tưởng này. Thực tế sẽ trông như thế nào? Con sẽ học ở trường địa phương hay trường quốc tế? Con có phải làm những năm cuối cấp trong một hệ thống giáo dục hoàn toàn xa lạ không?

Dad: Đó là điều chúng ta đã nghiên cứu. Có một trường quốc tế được đánh giá cao khoảng hai mươi phút từ khu vực chúng ta đang xem xét. Trường sử dụng chương trình học Mỹ, vì vậy các tín chỉ của con sẽ được chuyển và hồ sơ xin vào đại học của con sẽ không bị gián đoạn.

Daughter: Điều đó thực sự tốt hơn những gì con mong đợi. Con đã nghĩ con phải làm hệ thống Bồ Đào Nha từ đầu.

Mum: Chúng ta sẽ không làm điều gì khiến con bị tụt hậu về học thuật. Điều đó sẽ phá bỏ mục đích ban đầu. Cộng đồng trường quốc tế cũng thường khá gắn kết — nhiều trẻ em đã di chuyển xung quanh và hiểu điều đó như thế nào.

Daughter: Vậy là họ đều sẽ kỳ lạ vì đi du lịch nhiều và hơi không có chỗ đứng như con. Điều đó sẽ là sự phù hợp xã hội hoàn hảo hoặc hoàn toàn choáng ngợp.

Dad: Có lẽ cả hai một chút, thành thật mà nói. Ba không giả vờ là sẽ dễ dàng. Nhưng những điều khó khăn ban đầu thường trở thành những điều con nhìn lại như đã định hình mình.

Daughter: Con muốn thực sự đến thăm trước khi bất kỳ quyết định nào được đưa ra. Con chưa nói có hay không, nhưng con cần thấy nơi đó, thấy trường học, và cảm nhận xem đó có phải là nơi con thực sự có thể tưởng tượng sống hay không. Chúng ta có thể làm điều đó không?

Mum: Chắc chắn rồi. Đó luôn là một phần trong kế hoạch. Chúng ta đang nghĩ đến việc đi vào kỳ nghỉ học tháng Mười — điều đó cho chúng ta đủ thời gian để thăm trường, gặp một số gia đình, và có cảm nhận thực sự về nơi đó thay vì chỉ nhìn nó trực tuyến.

Dad: Và Zoe — bất cứ điều gì chúng ta quyết định, chúng ta quyết định cùng nhau. Đây không phải là điều chúng ta sẽ làm mà không có sự đồng lòng thực sự của con. Đó là lời hứa.

questions.prompt: Why does the mother say she wants to have the conversation properly rather than in passing?
options: She is worried the father has already decided without consulting anyone
She needs to make a decision within the week due to a property deadline
She is preparing Zoe for an announcement that has already been made
She wants all three family members to think through what the move would mean for each of them
questions.explanation: The mother says: 'I want us to talk properly about the Portugal idea — not just in passing, but actually sit down and think through what it would mean for all three of us.'
questions.hint: The mother is explicit about wanting to include all perspectives.

questions.prompt: What key change in their working lives does the father say makes the move possible?
options: Both parents now work remotely and are not tied to a specific location
He has been offered a job based in Portugal with a higher salary
They have saved enough to live for a year without working
Their employer has opened an office in Lisbon they can transfer to
questions.explanation: The father says: 'with both of us working remotely now, we're not tied to a location the way we were.'
questions.hint: The reason is about flexibility in where they work.

questions.prompt: What is Zoe's main expressed concern about the move?
options: She is worried about learning Portuguese before starting school
She is concerned about losing her friendships and disrupting her junior year
She is afraid the cost of living will actually be higher in Lisbon than at home
She is unhappy that her parents have been planning this without telling her
questions.explanation: Zoe says: 'Honestly? Leaving my friends. I know that sounds small… but those friendships took years to build, and I'm supposed to start junior year in September.'
questions.hint: She names a specific relationship cost and a specific academic timing concern.

questions.prompt: What argument does the father make using something Zoe has previously said?
options: She once expressed a desire to learn Portuguese
She mentioned wanting a lower-pressure academic environment
She has said she wants to study in Europe eventually
She expressed interest in spending a gap year abroad
questions.explanation: The father says: 'you've also said yourself that you'd like to study in Europe eventually.'
questions.hint: The father refers to a statement Zoe herself made before this conversation.

questions.prompt: How does Zoe respond to the language-learning argument for moving?
options: She says she already speaks basic Portuguese from school
She agrees it is a benefit but thinks it can happen through online courses instead
She says she would prefer to learn another language that is more widely spoken
She argues that learning a language and uprooting her social life are two very different things
questions.explanation: Zoe says: 'Learning a language and uprooting my entire social life are two very different things. You can learn a language without moving countries.'
questions.hint: Zoe separates the language benefit from the social cost.

questions.prompt: What challenge does Zoe raise about whether moving actually solves the family's problems?
options: She wonders whether they are running away from problems that might follow them anyway
She suggests the financial savings will be eaten up by relocation costs
She points out that Portuguese healthcare may not be as good as they believe
She argues that remote work is less stable than a fixed office job
questions.explanation: Zoe asks: 'is moving to another continent really the answer, or is it just running away from problems that might follow you anyway?'
questions.hint: Zoe questions the premise that relocation solves the underlying issues.

questions.prompt: How does the father respond to Zoe's question about running away from problems?
options: He dismisses the question and returns to the financial argument
He says some problems would follow them but the financial/housing pressure is structural and would change
He admits all their problems are structural and will follow them
He agrees it is a valid concern and says they should delay the decision
questions.explanation: The father says: 'Some problems would follow us and some wouldn't. The financial pressure is structural — it's tied to this specific housing market. That part would actually change.'
questions.hint: The father distinguishes between problems that are portable and those that are tied to a location.

questions.prompt: What financial benefit does the mother highlight that would improve the family's long-term situation?
options: They would qualify for Portuguese government grants for foreign residents
The father would receive a significant pay rise by working for a European company
They would be able to save properly for the first time in years, rather than just scraping by
Property prices in Portugal are low enough that they could buy a house outright
questions.explanation: The mother says: 'We'd be able to save properly for the first time in years. Not just scrape by, but actually build something.'
questions.hint: The mother contrasts two modes of managing money.

questions.prompt: What question does Zoe ask when she says she is willing to 'entertain the idea'?
options: Whether they would be near a beach or a city
Whether they have already signed a lease on an apartment
Whether she could bring her best friend to stay for the summer
Whether she would attend a local Portuguese school or an international school
questions.explanation: Zoe asks: 'Would I go to a local school or an international school? Would I be doing my final years in a completely foreign education system?'
questions.hint: Zoe's first practical question is about her education.

questions.prompt: What does the father say about the international school near the area they are considering?
options: It is a well-regarded school twenty minutes away that uses an American curriculum
It is a Portuguese school with a small English-medium stream
It is a UK-based school with a satellite campus that opened recently
It is the only English-medium school in the country and has a long waiting list
questions.explanation: The father says: 'There's a well-regarded international school about twenty minutes from the area we're looking at. It uses an American curriculum.'
questions.hint: The father gives both the proximity and the curriculum type.

questions.prompt: Why is the American curriculum at the school significant for Zoe?
options: It means Zoe will not need to take a Portuguese language test for entry
Her credits would transfer and her college applications would not be disrupted
It guarantees she will be taught by teachers who grew up in the United States
It allows her to graduate one year earlier than in the Portuguese system
questions.explanation: The father explains: 'your credits would transfer and your college applications wouldn't be disrupted.'
questions.hint: Two specific academic consequences are listed.

questions.prompt: What does Zoe say about the prospect of being at a school full of well-travelled, displaced students?
options: She is enthusiastic because she has always preferred international friends
She is worried it will make her feel more homesick for her friends at home
She says it would either be a perfect social fit or completely overwhelming
She thinks it sounds artificial and would prefer to attend a local school
questions.explanation: Zoe says: 'That's either going to be a perfect social fit or completely overwhelming.'
questions.hint: Zoe offers two contrasting possibilities without committing to either.

questions.prompt: What does the father say about difficult experiences and how they shape people?
options: He says difficult experiences are inevitable and should be accepted without complaint
He argues that moving abroad is not actually that difficult for teenagers
He says the difficulty will be short-lived because Zoe is adaptable
He says things that are hard initially often become the things you look back on as having defined you
questions.explanation: The father says: 'the things that are hard initially often become the things you look back on as having defined you.'
questions.hint: The father connects present difficulty to a future perspective.

questions.prompt: What condition does Zoe set before she is willing to consider the move seriously?
options: She wants to visit in person to see the place, the school, and whether she can imagine living there
She wants to spend six months in Portugal as a trial run before fully committing
She wants her parents to guarantee they will return if she is unhappy after one year
She wants to stay in the United States to finish her junior year before joining them
questions.explanation: Zoe says: 'I want to actually visit before any decision is made. I need to see the place, see the school, and feel whether it's somewhere I could actually imagine living.'
questions.hint: Zoe names three specific things she needs from the visit.

questions.prompt: When do the parents propose visiting Portugal?
options: In the summer before junior year starts
During the October school holidays
In December when Zoe's semester ends
As soon as the parents can take time off work
questions.explanation: The mother says: 'We were thinking of going over the school holidays in October.'

questions.prompt: What promise does the father make to Zoe at the end of the conversation?
options: That the family will definitely not move if Zoe remains opposed
That Zoe can fly home to visit her friends at least twice a year
That the decision will be made together, and they would not go without Zoe genuinely being on board
That they will give the move a one-year trial and reassess after twelve months
questions.explanation: The father says: 'Whatever we decide, we decide together. This isn't something we'd do without you genuinely being on board. That's a promise.'
questions.hint: The father's promise is about the decision-making process, not a specific outcome.

cloze.template: Mum: I want us to talk properly about the Portugal idea — not just {{b1}}, but actually sit down and think through what it would mean for all three of us. Dad: The cost of living there is genuinely significantly lower, and with both of us working {{b2}} now, we're not tied to a location the way we were. Daughter: I'm not exactly {{b3}}? I feel like my perspective kind of got lost somewhere. Mum: Your perspective matters a great deal. What's your biggest concern? Daughter: Honestly? Leaving my friends. Those friendships took years to build, and I'm supposed to start {{b4}} year in September. Dad: You've also said yourself that you'd like to study in Europe eventually. This could be an extraordinary {{b5}} for that — living somewhere new, learning a language, building a different kind of {{b6}}. Daughter: You can learn a language without moving countries. Mum: The {{b7}} levels we've both been carrying here — the housing costs, the healthcare — they've been affecting all of us, including you. Daughter: Is moving to another continent really the answer, or is it just {{b8}} from problems that might follow you anyway? Dad: Some problems would follow us and some wouldn't. The financial pressure is {{b9}} — it's tied to this specific housing market. That part would actually change. Mum: We'd be able to save properly for the first time in years — not just {{b10}}, but actually build something. Daughter: Let's say I entertain the idea. Would I go to a local school or an {{b11}} school? Dad: There's a well-regarded school about twenty minutes away. It uses an American {{b12}}, so your credits would transfer and your {{b13}} applications wouldn't be disrupted. Mum: The international school community tends to be quite {{b14}} — lots of kids who've moved around. Daughter: I want to actually visit before any decision is made. I need to feel whether it's somewhere I could actually imagine living. Can we do that? Dad: Whatever we decide, we decide {{b15}}. This isn't something we'd do without you genuinely being on board.

options: casually
briefly

by accident
blanks.explanation: The mother wants to discuss it properly, 'not just in passing' — not just as a casual remark.

options: part-time

blanks.explanation: The father says 'both of us working remotely' means they can live anywhere.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The father argues that the financial pressures the family faces are structural rather than personal, and that moving abroad is a genuine solution rather than escapism. To what extent do you think it is valid to make major life decisions — such as uprooting a family — in response to systemic economic pressures, and how should the interests of different family members be weighed against each other?

sentences.speaker: Mum
sentences.text: I want us to talk properly about the Portugal idea — not just in passing, but actually sit down and think through what it would mean for all three of us.

sentences.speaker: Dad
sentences.text: I think it's worth a serious conversation.

sentences.speaker: Dad
sentences.text: The cost of living there is genuinely significantly lower, and with both of us working remotely now, we're not tied to a location the way we were.

sentences.speaker: Daughter
sentences.text: Can I just say upfront that I'm not exactly thrilled?

sentences.speaker: Daughter
sentences.text: I know you've both been excited about this for months, but I feel like my perspective kind of got lost somewhere.

sentences.speaker: Mum
sentences.text: That's exactly why we're having this conversation, Zoe.

sentences.speaker: Mum
sentences.text: Your perspective matters a great deal.

sentences.speaker: Mum
sentences.text: What's your biggest concern?

sentences.speaker: Daughter
sentences.text: Honestly? Leaving my friends.

sentences.speaker: Daughter
sentences.text: I know that sounds small compared to all the things you're talking about, but those friendships took years to build, and I'm supposed to start junior year in September.

sentences.speaker: Dad
sentences.text: I hear you, and I don't want to minimise that.

sentences.speaker: Dad
sentences.text: But you've also said yourself that you'd like to study in Europe eventually.

sentences.speaker: Dad
sentences.text: This could be an extraordinary foundation for that — living somewhere new, learning a language, building a different kind of worldview.

sentences.speaker: Daughter
sentences.text: Learning a language and uprooting my entire social life are two very different things.

sentences.speaker: Daughter
sentences.text: You can learn a language without moving countries.

sentences.speaker: Mum
sentences.text: That's true, and I don't think the language point is the strongest argument.

sentences.speaker: Mum
sentences.text: But the quality of life question is real.

sentences.speaker: Mum
sentences.text: The stress levels we've both been carrying here — the housing costs, the healthcare — they've been affecting all of us, including you.

sentences.speaker: Daughter
sentences.text: I know you're stressed.

sentences.speaker: Daughter
sentences.text: I see it.

sentences.speaker: Daughter
sentences.text: But is moving to another continent really the answer, or is it just running away from problems that might follow you anyway?

sentences.speaker: Dad
sentences.text: That's a genuinely perceptive question, and I've asked myself the same thing.

sentences.speaker: Dad
sentences.text: I think the honest answer is: some problems would follow us and some wouldn't.

sentences.speaker: Dad
sentences.text: The financial pressure is structural — it's tied to this specific housing market.

sentences.speaker: Dad
sentences.text: That part would actually change.

sentences.speaker: Mum
sentences.text: We'd be able to save properly for the first time in years.

sentences.speaker: Mum
sentences.text: Not just scrape by, but actually build something.

sentences.speaker: Mum
sentences.text: That matters for all of us long-term — including your university options, Zoe.

sentences.speaker: Daughter
sentences.text: Okay, so let's say I entertain the idea.

sentences.speaker: Daughter
sentences.text: What would it actually look like?

sentences.speaker: Daughter
sentences.text: Would I go to a local school or an international school?

sentences.speaker: Daughter
sentences.text: Would I be doing my final years in a completely foreign education system?

sentences.speaker: Dad
sentences.text: That's something we've been researching.

sentences.speaker: Dad
sentences.text: There's a well-regarded international school about twenty minutes from the area we're looking at.

sentences.speaker: Dad
sentences.text: It uses an American curriculum, so your credits would transfer and your college applications wouldn't be disrupted.

sentences.speaker: Daughter
sentences.text: That's actually better than I expected.

sentences.speaker: Daughter
sentences.text: I assumed I'd have to do the Portuguese system from scratch.

sentences.speaker: Mum
sentences.text: We wouldn't do something that set you back academically.

sentences.speaker: Mum
sentences.text: That would defeat the purpose.

sentences.speaker: Mum
sentences.text: The international school community also tends to be quite close-knit — lots of kids who've moved around and understand what that's like.

sentences.speaker: Daughter
sentences.text: So they'd all be weirdly well-travelled and slightly displaced like me.

sentences.speaker: Daughter
sentences.text: That's either going to be a perfect social fit or completely overwhelming.

sentences.speaker: Dad
sentences.text: Probably a bit of both, honestly.

sentences.speaker: Dad
sentences.text: I'm not going to pretend it'll be easy.

sentences.speaker: Dad
sentences.text: But the things that are hard initially often become the things you look back on as having defined you.

sentences.speaker: Daughter
sentences.text: I want to actually visit before any decision is made.

sentences.speaker: Daughter
sentences.text: I'm not saying yes or no yet, but I need to see the place, see the school, and feel whether it's somewhere I could actually imagine living.

sentences.speaker: Daughter
sentences.text: Can we do that?

sentences.speaker: Mum
sentences.text: Absolutely.

sentences.speaker: Mum
sentences.text: That was always part of the plan.

sentences.speaker: Mum
sentences.text: We were thinking of going over the school holidays in October — that gives us enough time to visit the school, meet some families, and get a real sense of the place rather than just looking at it online.

sentences.speaker: Dad
sentences.text: And Zoe — whatever we decide, we decide together.

sentences.speaker: Dad
sentences.text: This isn't something we'd do without you genuinely being on board.

sentences.speaker: Dad
sentences.text: That's a promise.
